names.scientific_name: Sodium polyphosphates (E452i), Potassium polyphosphates (E452ii), Calcium polyphosphates (E452iv), Ammonium polyphosphates (E452v)

classification.sub_class: sequestrant, stabilizer, moisture retention agent, texturizer

natural_occurrence.description: Polyphosphates do not occur naturally. They are long-chain phosphate polymers produced synthetically through controlled heating and condensation of orthophosphate salts.

manufacturing.description: Produced by heating orthophosphates or pyrophosphates to temperatures above 600°C, causing progressive condensation to form long-chain polyphosphates. Chain length can be controlled by temperature, time, and starting material composition. Rapid cooling produces glassy polyphosphates, while slow cooling yields crystalline forms.

other_uses.cosmetics: Chelating agent and sequestrant in shampoos and toothpaste.
other_uses.industrial: Water treatment (prevents scale buildup), metal surface treatment, textile processing, oil drilling.
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Sequestrant and stabilizer in pharmaceutical formulations.
other_uses.household: Water softeners, detergents, boiler treatments (trade name Calgon for sodium hexametaphosphate).

eu.conditions: Quantum satis in many categories; specific limits apply due to concerns about total dietary phosphate intake.

usa.notes: Widely used in processed cheese and cola beverages

japan.japanese_regulation_name: ポリリン酸ナトリウム

safety_evaluations.jecfa_adi: 0-70 mg/kg bw/day (as phosphorus, for all phosphates)
safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: 40 mg/kg bw/day (as phosphorus, for all phosphates)

regulatory_history.event: EFSA's comprehensive evaluation of food phosphates expressed concern about high dietary intake from additives, particularly in Western processed food diets. Recommended monitoring and potential reduction.

fun_facts: Sodium hexametaphosphate (a polyphosphate) is sold as 'Calgon' water softener for washing machines - the same chemical family used in processed cheese
Polyphosphates can have chains ranging from just a few phosphate units to thousands - longer chains are better sequestrants (metal ion binders)
In evaporated milk, polyphosphates prevent the proteins from clumping together during heat processing and storage
The glassy, transparent form of sodium hexametaphosphate was historically called 'Graham's salt' after Thomas Graham who studied it in the 1830s
Polyphosphates gradually hydrolyze (break down) into simpler phosphates over time, especially in acidic or hot conditions - this is why cola beverages have a shelf life
